title: Hydrant Pressure Drop: Additional Attack Lines

scene.description: Engine 181 is connected to a hydrant. Static hydrant pressure is 80 PSI. One 1.75" attack line is opened, flowing 185 GPM. After the line is opened, residual hydrant pressure drops to 70 PSI. Using 20 PSI as the minimum residual pressure, how many additional 185 GPM attack lines can be added?

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop: Additional Attack Lines
problems.question: Engine 181 is connected to a hydrant. Static hydrant pressure is 80 PSI. One 1.75" attack line is opened, flowing 185 GPM. After the line is opened, residual hydrant pressure drops to 70 PSI. Using 20 PSI as the minimum residual pressure, how many additional 185 GPM attack lines can be added?
problems.inputLabel: How many additional lines can be added?
formulaBreakdown: This is a hydrant capacity scenario. The final answer is how many additional same-size attack lines can be added, not pump pressure.
Pressure drop after first line: 80 - 70 = 10 PSI.
Estimated Total Available GPM = Known Flow × sqrt((Static Pressure - Minimum Residual Pressure) / (Static Pressure - Current Residual Pressure)).
Round down because you cannot safely add part of an attack line.
Total same-size lines supported = 2, including the first line already flowing.
Friction loss = 0, nozzle pressure = 0, elevation = 0, appliance loss = 0, and pump pressure = 0 because no pump-pressure calculation is required.
problems.instructorExplanation: This problem estimates hydrant capacity from the pressure drop caused by one known flowing line. The final answer is the number of additional same-size attack lines that can be added while maintaining the selected minimum residual pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes: treating this like a pump-pressure problem, dividing pressure by GPM instead of using the square-root formula, forgetting to subtract the first flowing line, rounding up instead of down, or failing to maintain the 20 PSI minimum residual.

problems.title: Hydrant Drop: 90 Static to 70 Residual

formulaBreakdown: This is a hydrant capacity scenario. The final answer is additional same-size lines, not pump pressure.

problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes: treating this like a pump-pressure problem, dividing pressure by GPM instead of using the square-root formula, forgetting to subtract the first flowing line, rounding up instead of down, or failing to maintain the 20 PSI minimum residual.
